metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full co-registration work for `lock_lease_secs`: `ReconcilerConfig` dataclass field, `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer entry, `docs/config.md` row, env-var alias (`REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECS`), and a `test_config_file_wiring.py` test — matching the pattern established by all five existing reconciler config keys.
findings.finding: The new `[reconciler] lock_lease_secs` config key is proposed without specifying its co-registration across all the layers every other reconciler config key requires: `ReconcilerConfig` dataclass field, `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer entry, `docs/config.md` table row, env-var alias (pattern: `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECS`), and extension of `test_config_file_wiring.py`. All five existing reconciler keys (`jira_cli_timeout`, `lock_max_retries`, `deletion_probe_limit`, `id_guard_bypass_unsafe`, `max_acting_fraction`) are wired through all of these layers. Silently adding the key only as a constructor default or only in `ReconcilerConfig` without the rest creates a partial config-surface entry inconsistent with the established pattern.
findings.location: What section — 'Lease + cadence config: … read from a new [reconciler] key lock_lease_secs'; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's What/AC4: 'Register lock_lease_secs in ReconcilerConfig (default=120), _SECTIONS["reconciler"] (as_int, minimum=1), docs/config.md reconciler table, with env alias REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECS, and a test_config_file_wiring.py round-trip test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact config mechanism for `lock_lease_secs`: which file/class/section it belongs to, how it is parsed, and where the 120 s default is enforced — do not assume a `[reconciler]` section already exists.
findings.finding: The `[reconciler] lock_lease_secs` config key is asserted to exist (plan: 'read from a new `[reconciler]` key `lock_lease_secs`'), but zero hits for this key exist anywhere in `src/` — neither the key nor the `[reconciler]` config section appears in the codebase.
findings.location: What section: 'read from a new [reconciler] key lock_lease_secs (a constructor/param default, not a magic constant)' / AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (new `lock_lease_secs: int = 120` field on `ReconcilerConfig` + coercer in `_SECTIONS['reconciler']`), `src/rebar/config.py` (env-alias entry), and `docs/config.md` to the plan's explicit edit-target list.
findings.finding: The config key `lock_lease_secs` that the plan requires under `[reconciler]` is absent from `ReconcilerConfig` in `_config_schema.py` and from the `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer dict. Adding it requires edits to at least `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (the dataclass field and the coercer entry) and likely `src/rebar/config.py` (env-alias table) and `docs/config.md` — none of these files are listed in the plan's edit targets.
findings.location: AC line 4 / What section ('Lease + cadence config') / `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` lines 332–344 and 443–450
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py`, add `lock_lease_secs: int = 120` to `ReconcilerConfig` and `'lock_lease_secs': lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=10)` to `_SECTIONS['reconciler']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lock_lease_secs: int = 120` to `ReconcilerConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` and add its coercer (`lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=10)`) to `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` so the new config key is actually parsed and surfaced by `load_config()`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `[reconciler] lock_lease_secs` config key but never specifies adding it to `ReconcilerConfig` dataclass (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:332-344) or the `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer dict (lines 443-450). Without these additions, `load_config()` will silently ignore any `lock_lease_secs` value in `pyproject.toml` (the unknown-key warn path drops it), and the code will always use the constructor default — making the config key effectively inert and breaking the AC that requires it to be configurable.
findings.location: AC line: 'Lease duration + heartbeat cadence come from [reconciler] lock_lease_secs'; src/rebar/_config_schema.py:332-344, 443-450
findings.suggested_fix: In _config_schema.py: (1) add `lock_lease_secs: int = 120` to ReconcilerConfig; (2) add `'lock_lease_secs': lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=10)` to _SECTIONS['reconciler']. Then in _ref_lock.py read it via `load_config().reconciler.lock_lease_secs`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that C1 (524d-00fa-66ef-4432) delivers a ref-name-agnostic `_is_cas_mismatch` (i.e., removes the `'refs/heads/tickets' in args` hard-code at `_advisory_lock.py:425`) before C2 ships, or define a C2-local CAS wrapper that correctly classifies exit-128 on `refs/reconciler/*` without relying on C1's generalization being complete.
findings.finding: _is_cas_mismatch (advisory_lock.py:425) hard-codes 'refs/heads/tickets' in the CAS-race discriminator, so any git update-ref exit-128 on refs/reconciler/* will not be recognized as a retryable CAS race and will propagate as an unclassified error. This plan's renew() depends on _cas_advance_with_retry (or a derived wrapper) correctly classifying a CAS mismatch on refs/reconciler/lock, but the generalization of _is_cas_mismatch is owned by C1 (open, status: open). The plan treats this as already resolved but it is not yet delivered.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper _cas_advance_with_retry'; _advisory_lock.py:415–426
findings.suggested_fix: In _ref_lock.py, define a local `_is_reconciler_cas_mismatch` that checks for `'update-ref' in args and 'refs/reconciler/' in ' '.join(args)` and `exc.returncode == 128`, and use it in a thin local retry loop rather than calling _cas_advance_with_retry until C1's generalization is merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete signal type for `renew()` lease-loss (e.g. 'raise `LeaseLostError(...)`, a new exception subclassing `Exception` defined in `_ref_lock.py`') so the C2/C3 interface contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The 'lease lost' signal is described as 'raises/returns a defined lease lost signal' without specifying whether it is a named exception class, a sentinel return value, or an error enum. An executor must invent the interface, which risks incompatibility with C3 (the orchestrator that consumes it).
findings.location: What section — 'renew() failure contract'; AC4 — 'raises a defined "lease lost" signal'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'renew() raises `LeaseLostError` (a new exception class in `_ref_lock.py`) on CAS rejection; C3 catches `LeaseLostError` to trigger pass-abort.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether C1 ships a stub/skeleton `renew()` that this plan *completes* (adding fence-bump and lease-lost semantics), or whether C1 ships no `renew()` at all and this plan is its sole author — then update the dependency block and AC1 to reflect exactly one source of truth.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously states that `renew()` is introduced by C1 ('renew(), the blob schema (heartbeat_ns, fence), and the CAS helpers are introduced by C1') and that this plan's own `_ref_lock.py` 'gains `renew()`' (AC1) plus describes its full implementation semantics in the What section. An implementer reading the What section will write `renew()` from scratch; one reading the dependency statement will treat it as pre-existing — these directions are incompatible.
findings.location: What section — dependency block ('renew()…are introduced by C1') vs. What section bullet ('`renew()` (heartbeat) CAS-updates…') vs. AC1 ('_ref_lock.py gains `renew()`')
findings.suggested_fix: If C1 introduces only the blob schema and CAS helpers (not `renew()` itself), remove `renew()` from the C1 dependency clause; AC1 stays as-is. If C1 does introduce a partial `renew()`, restate AC1 as 'extend `renew()` to bump `fence` and raise on CAS rejection' and describe only the delta.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify C1 (524d-00fa-66ef-4432) has merged and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py` exists with `renew()`, `heartbeat_ns`, `fence`, and CAS helpers before this plan's work begins; add an explicit 'merge gate' precondition to the plan.
findings.finding: `_ref_lock.py` is asserted to already exist as a C1 artifact this plan 'extends', but the file is absent from the codebase — C1 ticket 524d-00fa-66ef-4432 is still 'open' and its deliverable has not landed. All plan assertions that treat `_ref_lock.py` as a pre-existing module are unverifiable.
findings.location: What section: 'Builds directly on C1' / 'Lives in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py (extends C1)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preconditions block: 'This plan may not begin implementation until C1 (524d-00fa-66ef-4432) is merged and _ref_lock.py is present with the contracted blob schema and CAS helpers.' This converts an implicit dependency into an enforced gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `renew()` is a C1 deliverable (already present when C2 begins) or a C2 deliverable (added by this plan); AC1 and the What section currently contradict each other on this point.
findings.finding: `renew()` is asserted to be introduced by C1 and already callable from this plan's code, but zero grep hits for `def renew` exist anywhere in the codebase — the function does not exist at review time.
findings.location: What section: 'renew() (heartbeat) CAS-updates the blob's heartbeat_ns and bumps fence' / AC1: '_ref_lock.py gains renew()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that when C1 lands, the blob schema includes exactly `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}` as described; add a schema-version or schema-reference assertion to the plan so a C1 schema change is caught at plan-review time rather than at runtime.
findings.finding: The blob schema fields `heartbeat_ns` and `fence` are asserted to be 'introduced by C1', but neither field exists anywhere in the codebase — zero grep hits for either name in `src/`.
findings.location: What section: 'the blob schema (heartbeat_ns, fence) … are introduced by C1'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 'blocked on C1 merge' precondition gate: confirm `_ref_lock.py` exists with `acquire`/`release`/`read`, the `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}` blob schema, and the generalized CAS helpers before any C2 implementation begins.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py` does not exist in the codebase — it is the primary edit target of this plan, but parent ticket C1 (524d-00fa-66ef-4432) is still `open` and unimplemented. The plan states the module and its symbols (`renew()`, `heartbeat_ns`, `fence`, CAS helpers) are 'introduced by C1', yet no such file or symbols exist anywhere under `src/`.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1 / file path `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete exception type for the 'lease lost' signal in the plan (e.g., `LeaseLostError(ReconcileLockError)` as a new subclass, or explicitly reuse `ReconcileLockError` with a distinguishing attribute), so the AC and C3's catch clause have an unambiguous contract.
findings.finding: The plan names the 'lease lost' signal that `renew()` raises but does not specify its type — whether it is an existing exception (e.g., `ReconcileLockError` from C1), a new subclass, or a new standalone exception. Without a concrete type, the C3 orchestrator (which must catch exactly this signal to abort the pass) cannot be implemented consistently, and the AC ('raises a defined lease lost signal') is not verifiable without knowing what 'defined' means.
findings.location: What section: 'renew() failure contract'; AC line: 'renew() on CAS rejection raises a defined "lease lost" signal rather than retrying'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and ADR) that `fence` serves only as a **progress witness** in C2 (contender checks fence stability); if the fencing-token semantic (downstream write guard) is intended for a future child, name that child explicitly, or drop the 'fencing token' label to avoid implying a guarantee the current design does not provide.
findings.finding: The `fence` field is described as both a 'progress witness' and a 'fencing token'. Within C2 the progress-witness role is fully justified and consumed (contender checks fence stability across one lease interval). But the fencing-token semantic — where a monotonically increasing token is passed to downstream storage systems so they can reject writes from stale lock-holders — has zero consumers anywhere in the C1/C2/C3 plan chain. Labelling fence a 'fencing token' without a downstream enforcer that checks it is cargo-culted vocabulary from Kleppmann-style distributed systems literature, implying a correctness guarantee the design does not actually provide.
findings.location: What section — 'renew() … bumps fence (fence += 1) … Its consumer is precisely the progress-witness rule above'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the What section (and AC1) whether `fence` is a *progress witness only* (the contender uses it to detect stale holders) or a full *fencing token* (presented to a downstream resource server); if only the former, remove or qualify the 'fencing token' label to avoid overstating the guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'fencing token' to describe the `fence` field but conflates two distinct semantics from the distributed-systems literature: (1) a *progress witness* (detecting that the holder made no forward progress across a lease interval — the plan's actual use) and (2) a *fencing token* in Kleppmann's sense (a monotonically increasing number presented to a protected resource to guard against stale writes). These are related but not identical. The plan does not state which guarantee `fence` is intended to provide beyond progress-witnessing, leaving the 'fencing token' label as an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: What section — '`renew()` (heartbeat) CAS-updates the blob's `heartbeat_ns` and **bumps `fence`** (`fence += 1`)'; AC line — 'Acceptance Criteria AC1: fence is a monotonic int (progress witness + fencing token)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'progress witness + fencing token' to 'progress witness (monotonic sequence number; a contender reads fence before and after one lease interval — no change means no progress); it is NOT a resource-server fencing token in the Kleppmann sense, since no downstream server validates it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the exact heartbeat cadence formula (e.g. `max(1, lease_secs // 3)`) in the AC so an executor has no discretion to fill in the formula.
findings.finding: The heartbeat cadence is specified as '~lease/3' (with a tilde), leaving the exact formula unresolved — an executor must independently decide whether it is floor(lease/3), round(lease/3), or something else, and whether fractional seconds are truncated or rounded. This is a defaultable gap but could cause drift between independent implementations or tests that assert a specific value.
findings.location: What section — 'heartbeat every ~lease/3'; AC4 — 'cadence ~lease/3'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '~lease/3' with 'floor(lease_secs / 3), minimum 1 s' everywhere it appears (both in the What section and AC4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'Extend the C1 ADR' with a conditional: 'Once C1 merges and its ADR exists under docs/adr/, extend it with the lease/heartbeat/fence contract; if C1's ADR path differs from expected, update the reference here.'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Extend the C1 ADR with the lease/heartbeat/fence contract', but no ref-lock ADR exists in `docs/adr/` — C1's ADR deliverable has not landed (C1 is open). The assertion treats a future artifact as a present extension target.
findings.location: What section (last bullet): 'Extend the C1 ADR with the lease/heartbeat/fence contract'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC (e.g. `pytest src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/tests/test_ref_lock.py -k lease`) and name the test file/class where the regression tests for AC2 and AC3 will live.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (e.g. `pytest src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/tests/test_ref_lock.py`) is named for any AC. 'Non-integration suite green' is the only verification reference but it is unanchored — no test file path, no pytest invocation, no CI job name. An agent completing the work cannot produce unambiguous evidence of AC1–AC5 without inferring the test location.
findings.location: AC5 — 'Non-integration suite green'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a paragraph (or ADR stub) in the C2 design or the referenced ADR explicitly comparing the chosen relative-duration approach against at least one alternative (e.g., absolute-timestamp + NTP-skew-budget expiry, or Kleppmann's canonical recipe), and note why the DynamoDB lock client pattern is the right fit for a git-ref-backed blob with no native TTL support.
findings.finding: The plan names 'the DynamoDB-lock-client pattern' as its design authority for relative-duration leases but does not demonstrate that well-established alternatives (etcd session TTLs, Chubby/ZooKeeper lease sessions, Redis SETNX+TTL, or Kleppmann's canonical 'How to do distributed locking' relative-duration recipe) were considered and ruled out. The translation from DynamoDB (which has native storage-layer TTL) to a git-ref blob (which has no TTL) is non-trivial and the adaptation is not grounded in any cited prior art.
findings.location: What section — 'Relative-duration, skew-proof lease (DynamoDB-lock-client pattern)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR section C1 extends (or a new C2 addendum)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether modifying `renew()`'s contract (fence-bump + raise-on-rejection) is a breaking change to C1 consumers, and if so add an expand-contract note (e.g., C1 ships the final contract from the start, or this plan is gated until C3 is updated).
findings.finding: This plan modifies the behaviour of C1's `renew()` (adding `fence += 1` and a lease-lost raise-on-rejection contract) without acknowledging that C3 or any other C1 consumer may already call the C1-defined `renew()` under its original contract. No expand-contract sequence or rollback path for the changed `renew()` signature/contract is described.
findings.location: What section — '`renew()` failure contract' bullet and dependency block
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note: 'C1 intentionally ships `renew()` as a no-op stub so C3 is written against this plan's final contract; no expand-contract migration is needed.' Or, if C1 ships a real implementation, list the exact caller update required before this plan is merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Distinguish transient DynamoDB errors from definitive CAS rejection in `renew()`: specify retry-with-backoff for retryable errors (e.g., throttles, transient I/O) before raising 'lease lost', and document the error taxonomy so C3 can react appropriately.
findings.finding: Transient DynamoDB errors (network timeout, throttling, ProvisionedThroughputExceededException) during `renew()` are not distinguished from a genuine CAS rejection — both would surface as 'lease lost', incorrectly aborting a healthy pass-holder when the store is merely slow.
findings.location: AC line 4 / What section — '`renew()` failure contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small retry budget (e.g., 3 attempts with exponential backoff, total ≤ lease/6) for retryable DynamoDB exceptions inside `renew()`. Only raise 'lease lost' on a definitive ConditionalCheckFailedException. Surface other irrecoverable errors as a distinct exception (e.g., `LockStoreUnavailable`) so C3 can choose to abort-with-requeue rather than treat it as a hostile steal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for the lock blob: specify who deletes or TTL-expires it (holder on clean release, C3 on pass completion, or a DynamoDB TTL attribute), and add this to the ADR extension so the full CREATE→UPDATE→RETIRE lifecycle is complete.
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup path is defined for the lock blob — it is unclear who deletes it (or marks it inactive) after reconciliation completes, and whether abandoned blobs from crashed passes accumulate indefinitely in the store.
findings.location: What section — module scope / lifecycle; AC lines (none reference cleanup)
findings.suggested_fix: Have the holder perform a conditional delete (CAS on current OID) on clean pass exit; also set a DynamoDB TTL attribute to `now + 2*lease_secs` on every write so orphaned blobs self-expire even without an explicit delete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale sentence to the plan explaining why the relative-duration lease pattern is chosen for this codebase (e.g., 'CI runners share no synchronized clock source and git itself provides the only shared atomic state, making relative-duration expiry the only skew-proof option without external infrastructure').
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why the DynamoDB-lock-client relative-duration lease pattern (over a git bare-ref blob) is the right fit for this codebase — it names the pattern but provides no argument for its selection over other liveness-detection approaches within the git-ref substrate (e.g., epoch-stamped monotonic fence comparison alone, or a server-side TTL on the ref). Without a stated rationale, implementers and reviewers lack the design intent needed to make coherent trade-off decisions during implementation.
findings.location: What section: 'Relative-duration, skew-proof lease (DynamoDB-lock-client pattern)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
